coredata.dc:title: High sensitivity, low-cost, and disposability: A novel screen-printed electrode developed for direct electrochemical detection of the antibiotic ceftriaxone
coredata.dc:creator: Silva, Francisco Walison Lima
Name, Luccas L.
Tiba, Daniel Y.
Braz, Bernardo Ferreira
Santelli, Ricardo Erthal
Canevari, Thiago C.
Cincotto, Fernando Henrique
coredata.dc:description: This study describes the development of a novel disposable and low-cost electrochemical platform for detecting the antibiotic ceftriaxone. The screen-printed electrode has been modified with a novel hybrid nanostructure containing silicon oxide (SiO2), zirconium oxide (ZrO2), and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (Cdot-N). Different techniques like Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy characterized the hybrid nanostructure used in the sensor surface modifier material. The hybrid nanostructure showed an excellent synergistic effect that contributed to the oxidation reaction of ceftriaxone. The screen-printed electrode modified with SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N nanostructure presented high sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.2 nmol L−1 in the linear range of 0.0078–40.02 μmol L−1. The measurements have been performed by square wave voltammetry technique. Studies on real samples of synthetic urine, urine, and tap water showed 95%–105% recovery without applying any sample pretreatment. The sensor demonstrated excellent selectivity in the antibiotic ceftriaxone determination in the presence of possible interferences cationic, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, and biological, glucose, caffeine, uric acid, and ascorbic acid. The developed sensor becomes a selective, sensitive, and applicable tool in determining the antibiotic ceftriaxone.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ceftriaxone
Electrochemical sensor
Low-cost
Disposable
Quantum dots. hybrid nanostructure

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: All the authors thank the Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior (CAPES) , Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq) and Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado do Rio de Janeiro (FAPERJ) (Cincotto Proc. E-26/202.696/2019 , Proc. E-26/010.002267/2019 and E-26/210.304/2022 ) for the financial support. Thiago Cruz Canevari thanks FINEP and Mackpesquisa for the financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterials TEM micrographs: (a) Cdot-N; (b) Cdot-N with an applied FFT filter; (c) SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N nanocomposites, (d) SiO2/Cdot-N and (e) SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N nanocomposites with two different phases: (f) light contrast nanoparticles EDS spectra; (g) high contrast nanoparticles EDS spectra.
(a) Raman spectra of carbon quantum dots and (b) FTIR spectra of SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N nanocomposites and Cdot-N.
SWV (A) and CV (B) voltammograms of 25 μmol L−1 CFX in 0.1 mol L−1 BR buffer (pH 7): (a) electrolyte (b) SPE, (c) SPE/Cdot-N, (d) SPE/SiO2/Cdot-N and (e) SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N.
Effect of pH on E (V) (a) and i (μA) (b) of CFX 25 μmol L−1 in BR 0.1 mol L−1 buffer solution with SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor.
SWV voltammograms at the concentrations: (a) 0.0078; (b) 0.09; (c) 0.61; (d) 2.50; (e) 5.00 (f) 10.05; (g) 20.1; (h) 40.02 μmol L−1. (a-inserted) correlation plot between [CFX] and ip.
Fabrication and modification procedure for screen-printed electrodes.
Proposed reaction mechanism for the oxidation of the aminothiazole group of ceftriaxone [51].
Comparison of SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor with other platforms electrochemical for CFX determination.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: PtNps/MWCNT/GCE - glassy carbon electrode-platinum nanoparticle decorated multiwalled carbon nanotube.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CAC-PU/PtE - sensor based on caffeic acid and chitosan-containing polyurethane films modified platinum electrode.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: RGO/MIP/PDPA/GCE – glassy carbon-molecular imprinted polymer – poly-diphenylamine and reduced graphene oxide.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: g
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: NiFe2O4-NPs/GCE - nickel ferrite nanoparticles modified glassy carbon electrode.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: h
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N – screen-printed electrode – nitrogen doped-carbon quantum dots (Cdot-N), silicon oxide (SiO2) and Zirconium oxide (ZrO2).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analyses of the CFX determination in different matrices.

head.ce:title: High sensitivity, low-cost, and disposability: A novel screen-printed electrode developed for direct electrochemical detection of the antibiotic ceftriaxone

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Universidade Federal Do Rio de Janeiro

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study describes the development of a novel disposable and low-cost electrochemical platform for detecting the antibiotic ceftriaxone. The screen-printed electrode has been modified with a novel hybrid nanostructure containing silicon oxide (SiO2), zirconium oxide (ZrO2), and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (Cdot-N). Different techniques like Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy characterized the hybrid nanostructure used in the sensor surface modifier material. The hybrid nanostructure showed an excellent synergistic effect that contributed to the oxidation reaction of ceftriaxone. The screen-printed electrode modified with SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N nanostructure presented high sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.2 nmol L−1 in the linear range of 0.0078–40.02 μmol L−1. The measurements have been performed by square wave voltammetry technique. Studies on real samples of synthetic urine, urine, and tap water showed 95%–105% recovery without applying any sample pretreatment. The sensor demonstrated excellent selectivity in the antibiotic ceftriaxone determination in the presence of possible interferences cationic, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, and biological, glucose, caffeine, uric acid, and ascorbic acid. The developed sensor becomes a selective, sensitive, and applicable tool in determining the antibiotic ceftriaxone.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Monitoring of antibiotics present in many pharmaceuticals is very much necessary due to being a significant public health issue [1,2]. The growing development and consumption of drugs have been increasing the number of substances within the broad spectrum of emerging contaminants, contributing to environmental contamination and mainly harmful effects on human health. According to Pal et al. [3] it is predicted that the pharmaceutical sector will grow by about 160% by the year 2030 to meet population growth needs. Antibiotic residues have already been founded in aquatic environments due to excessive manufacturing, disposal, and inadequate consumption [4]. Persisting this reality, it is expected that by the year 2050, about 10 million people will die yearly due to antibiotic-resistant genes [5]. This may aggravate environmental and health problems.

In this perspective, ceftriaxone sodium (CFX) (Fig. 1 ), a third-generation semisynthetic parenteral antibiotic, which belongs to the cephalosporin group whose bactericidal action consists of blocking the formation and division of the cell wall [6]. The pharmacokinetic profile of CFX makes it suitable for outpatient use as it has favorable characteristics for daily administration, a broad antibacterial spectrum, and patient compliance, as well as cost-advantageous considerations [7,8]. Furthermore, according to the study by Ducan et al. [8] the restriction on the use of cephalosporins is becoming the target of antimicrobial stewardship campaigns to control the resistance of target organisms to this group of antibiotics.

Various methods for monitoring CFX in different matrices are reported in the literature, for example, spectrophotometric [9], chromatographic [10–12 10–12 10–12], and capillary electrophoresis [13]. These methods, although usual, have limitations such as expensive instrumentation, rigorous protocols, complex sample preparation, high cost of analysis, and time-consuming procedures [14,15]. Electrochemical methods for CFX detection are advantageous due to their present low-cost, simple and rapid fabrication, in situ applicability and high sensitivity [16–23] with no need for sample pretreatment compared to other routine analytical techniques [24].

In the literature, different modified electrodes are presented for determination of antibiotic CFX. Most of the developed sensors were based on the use of glass carbon electrodes (GCE), modified with, for example, carbon nanotubes (CNT), metallic nanoparticles (MNps), reduced graphene oxide (RGO), and magnetic materials such as Ferrite (Fe2O4) [19,22,25,26]. Other materials are the basis for sensors, such as graphite paste electrode modified with copper hexacyanoferrate [18], platinum electrode (PtE) [20] and Hanging mercury drop electrode (HMDE) [27]. In addition, although electrochemical methods present favorable characteristics when compared to traditional methods, it is noted that sensors based on GCE, PGE and PtE present unfavorable characteristics such as non-disposable, electrode poisoning, preventing the reproducibility and sensitivity of the sensor through adsorption during the analyzes correcting the need for constant cleaning per analysis [28,29]. For HMDE, the high toxicity of mercury is highlighted, making its use unfeasible.

These characteristics the electrochemical methods, especially applicability, are maximized when using chemically modified screen-printed electrodes (SPE), which have easy handling and disposability after analysis [28–30]. In general, SPEs stand out in the field of electroanalytic for their easy operation, versatility, low-cost, portability, the possibility of extending the manufacturing process, low-volumes sample per analysis and does not have the disadvantages of non-disposable electrodes [31,32].

Nanomaterials such as metallic oxides, silicon oxide (SiO2) and zirconium oxide (ZrO2), for example, and carbon quantum dots (Cdot) present several applications [33–35]. These nanometric structure materials stand out as surface chemical modifiers. Recent studies show the application of nanomaterials as modifiers of electrochemical sensors and biosensors because they have unique structural properties, high mobility of charge carriers, micrometric structure whit high conductivity and high surface-to-volume ratio [26].

In this perspective, this study presents the development of screen-printed electrode modified with a novel hybrid nanostructure denominated SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor for detection of the CFX antibiotic. The developed electrochemical platform showed high sensitivity and selectivity to the oxidation process of CFX antibiotic with a low-volume sample consumption (50 μL per analyses) providing minimum waste generation.

The analytical grade reagents, ceftriaxone sodium (CFX), boric acid, phosphoric acid, acetic acid, potassium hexacyanoferrate (II), potassium hexacyanoferrate (III), potassium chloride, oleylamine technical grade 70%, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), and Zirconium chloride (IV) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich-Germany, absolute ethanol, and molecular sieves 0.3 nm were acquired from Metrohm. All solutions used in the electrochemical analyses were prepared with ultrapure water obtained in a Merck Milli-Q® Reference system (resistivity>18.2 MΩ cm). The CFX standard solution was prepared at a concentration of 1.0 mmol L−1, which was used to prepare all CFX solutions used in the subsequent analyses by dilution with 0.1 mol L−1 BR buffer. The pH values of the supporting electrolyte were adjusted with concentrated sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid. The solution of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- 5 mmol L−1 was prepared in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl.

Equipment for synthesis includes an ultrasonic bath (EcoSonics) and Potentiostat/Galvanostat PGSTAT101/Autolab from Metrohm. FTIR spectra with a Shimadzu (IRAffinity-1), ATR module, and MIRacle ATR with a diamond crystal. Raman spectra with a 532 nm laser (1.8 mW) equipped in a WITec alpha 300 R confocal Raman microscope. TEM micrography has been obtained using a JEOL JEM 2100. The samples were prepared on CF300–Cu-50 carbon film, 300 Mesh, and copper grids from EMS. The electroanalytical analyses of the CFX and performance of the proposed sensor were performed on an Autolab PGSTAT101 potentiostat/galvanostat (Metrohm) controlled by NOVA 2.1 software (Eco Chemie). The SPE used was developed with a Silhouette Cameo 6 cutting printer. Transparency sheet for laser printers, vinyl adhesive sheet (purchased in local stores) as a base for constructing the mask to be painted later with carbon (C2030519P4), and finally, the reference electrode was painted with silver chloride (C2130905D3) were obtained from Gwent Electronic Materials Ltd. (United Kingdom).

Cdot-N has been synthesized using the reference Cesena et al. [36] with minor changes. In a 200 mL beaker containing 50 mL of oleylamine, 4 mL of NaOH solution was added, yielding a final concentration of 0.16 mol L−1. The mixture was sonicated using an ultrasound bath for approximately 20 min. After that, two 4 cm2 platinum (Pt) electrodes were inserted into the beaker with a calomel reference electrode. The distance between the platinum electrodes was maintained at approximately 1.5 cm. After this procedure, a constant potential of 6.5 V was applied to the platinum electrodes with a current of 100 mA. The Cdot-N was collected after eight working hours. Scheme S1 (supplementary material) shows the assay setup and proposed reaction.

In a 100 mL bottle, 26.3 mL of absolute ethanol dried on the molecular sieve, 0.6 mL of TEOS, and 1.5 mL of the Zr(OEt)xCl(4-x) should be mixed and fixed to an ultrasound bath. The system should be set for one working hour. Once the process is started, 1.5 mL of Cdot-N should be added. After 1 h, the mixture should be ventilated for one week as a curing process. The zirconium ethoxide mixture was prepared according to what has been shown by Panice et al. [37], with modifications: in a 250 mL round bottom flask, 3.26 g of zirconium chloride (IV) was mixed 60 mL of absolute ethanol dried on a molecular sieve. The procedure was carried out inside a glove box with a nitrogen atmosphere. For comparison, the SiO2/Cdot-N hybrid material was also synthesized following the same procedure without the addition of the precursor Zr(OEt)xCl(4-x).

The screen-printed electrodes were produced following the process: adhere the vinyl adhesive sheet to the transparency sheet for laser printers (A4 size) and take it to the cutting printer; detach the vinyl sheet to form the mask, paint it with carbon ink and take it to the oven at 50 °C for 30 min to dry; apply the silver ink in the space reserved for the reference electrode and take it to the range at 50 °C for 10 min. After that, detach the excess adhesive mask (vinyl sheet) and cut out the screen-printed electrodes. Next, they inserted a vinyl sheet of tape over the electrode contacts (this vinyl tape can be inserted before performing the analysis, that is, after the modification process). A suspension containing 1 mg of SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N in 2 mL of ethanol was prepared for SPE modification. 5 μL of this suspension was deposited on the surface of the SPE working electrode after being exposed to ultrasound for 20 min and stirred for 5 s, respectively. The same process was carried out for the preparation of the SPE/Cdot-N and SPE/SiO2/Cdot-N sensors. Drying of the modified electrodes occurred at room temperature. The fabrication and modification process of the SPEs is depicted in Scheme 1 .

The SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor was applied to determine CFX in synthetic urine, real urine, and tap water. The synthetic urine samples were prepared following the procedures presented in the literature [38,39]. Four samples were prepared for analysis. CFX standard was added to samples A, B and C, whose final concentrations were 4.75, 5.29 and 0.025 μmol L−1, respectively. Sample D was prepared without the addition of CFX to evaluate the absence of previous contamination of the synthetic urine (blank). The urine real used in the analysis was collected from a healthy male (30 years old) with permission to employ him in this analytical study. The real urine sample was collected in a clean and sterilized bottle (morning period), which was later allocated in the refrigerator at two °C. Analyzes were performed between 1 and 2 h after collection. Four samples were prepared for analysis. CFX standard was added to samples E, F and G, whose final concentrations were 5.82, 6.35 and 0.021 μmol L−1, respectively. Sample H without the addition of CFX (blank). For tap water analysis, samples I, J and K were prepared with added CFX up to the concentration of 5.10, 10.25 and 0.042 μmol L−1, respectively, and L without added CFX. All real samples were prepared in 0.1 mol L−1 BR buffer (pH 5).

Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (Cdot-N), SiO2/Cdot-N and SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N hybrid nanostructure micrographs can be found in Fig. 2 . As can be seen, after the application of a filter based on a Fourier transforms, led to a clear image regarding the nanomaterial. Furthermore, the calculated interplane spaces, about 0.32 and 0.2 nm, sustain the idea that these are graphitic carbon quantum dots [40,41].

The SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N hybrid nanostructure can be found in both Fig. 2(c) and (e). The micrographs confirm two distinct regions in this hybrid nanostructure: The larger particles with a higher contrast and the much smaller particles with a lighter difference. One can suggest that both phases act very synergistically, leading to a tight packing, in which interparticle voids could be strongly reduced due to this observed size discrepancy. It is noteworthy to mention that no graphitic structure could be observed in these micrographs. So can assume that the Cdot-N has been fully encapsulated, and therefore, the observation of such nanostructures was eclipsed by the higher contrast materials that compose the studied hybrid nanostructure. These contrast differences are also obtained for the SiO2/Cdot-N hybrid material (Fig. 2(d)) but at a lower intensity.

Furthermore, an EDS analysis has been collected regarding both regions in Fig. 2(e) to comprehend the phases' compositions fully. As a result, it is possible to confirm that the smaller particles are mainly silica (Fig. 2(f)), whereas larger particles are composed of silicon and zirconium (Fig. 2(g)). This leads to the understanding that the excess feed of TEOS could lead to two main condensation mechanisms: the growth of smaller silica “seeds” and the other zirconia-promoted overgrown nanomaterial.

The carbon quantum dots were also analyzed with Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 3 (a)). It is possible to observe bands at around 1360 and 1600 cm−1, which are D and G bands [42]. This is by the observed micrographs and interplane spaces shown in Fig. 1.

FTIR spectrums of the prepared hybrid nanostructure can be found in Fig. 3(b). The wide band between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 can be related to hydroxyls and NH2 vibrations [43]. The contribution the N–H stretching they refer to the amino groups originating from oleylamine present in Cdot-N as demonstrated in Scheme S1. According to Innocenzi [44], the contribution of hydroxyl groups (O–H stretching) comes from factors such as, for example, the possible presence of residual ethanol, molecularly adsorbed water and hydroxyl of silanols adsorbed on the surface of silicon oxide. Vibrations at 2920 and 2850 cm−1 can be assigned to C–H stretching [36]. Vibrations around 1370 and 1650 cm−1 can be assigned to carboxyl and sp2 hybridized carbon [45]. Considering the SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N hybrid nanostructure, the band around 1030 cm−1 is given to Si–O–Si stretches, and 960 cm−1 is assigned to silanol groups [46].

SPE, SPE/SiO2/Cdot-N and SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N were used to evaluate the electrochemical behavior of 25 μmol L−1 of CFX. The comparative voltammograms obtained by square wave voltammetry (SWV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques have been presented in Fig. 4 . The analyses were performed in 0.1 mol L−1 BR buffer (pH 7), in the potential range of 0 to +1.0 V, with step potential at 5 mV, amplitude modulation at 25 mV, frequency at 25 (Hz) (SWV) and scan rate 50 mVs−1 (CV). All analyses were carried out with a total volume of 50 μL in the electrochemical cell, added to the surface of the electrode developed in the form of a drop.

As observed in Fig. 4A(a), the supporting electrolyte showed no responses to electrochemical processes with the sensor SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N. Similar analyzes were performed for the sensors SPE, SPE/Cdot-N and SPE/SiO2/Cdot-N, the results are shown in Fig. S2(a). However, in Fig. 4A(b), 4A(c), 4A(d) and 4A(e) whose addition of CFX was performed, electrochemical responses were observed in two electrooxidation peaks, whose Ep of peak I and peak II were about 0.52 and 0.73 V. Furthermore, the modifications of the SPE with the materials Cdot-N, SiO2/Cdot-N and SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N provided positive variations of the signal response to the oxidation of CFX when compared of the SPE bare. These synergistic contributions of the modifying materials to the increase in the electroanalytical signal were also observed in analyzes using the cv technique, which additionally demonstrated the irreversibility of the process (Fig. 4B). In addition, Fig. S2(b) shows the performance of the SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor by the CV technique in a range from −1 to +1 V in the absence and presence of antibiotic CFX, demonstrating that there is no response to electroreduction potentials <0 V.

The high performance of the SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N hybrid material in the electrooxidation do CFX antibiotic demonstrate that the zirconium oxide and Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots are dispersed under the silica coating, which makes them electrochemically conductive nanocomposites. Thus, it is inferred that the active sites of ZrO2 and Cdot-N are directly involved in interactions with the CFX antibiotic. The electrocatalytic contribution of zirconium oxide stands out for its chemical stability, amphoteric capacity, high affinity for oxygen-containing functional groups and increase in the number of Lewis acid sites in the material [47]. Summarized, the enhanced performance of SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor occurs due to the synergistic contribution of zirconium oxide and Cdot-N well distributed by the silica coating that facilitates the interaction of the CFX with the surface of the proposed sensor.

Analyses were performed on the solution of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- 5 mmol L−1 in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl, which demonstrated that the electroactive area of the SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor is possibly higher than that of the SPE bare by the variation of the anodic peak and cathodic peak current as presented in Fig. S3. To confirm the increase in the electroactive area, analyses were performed with the solution of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- 5 mmol L−1 in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl at different scan rates (10 - 125 mVs−1) (Fig. S4) and using the Randles-SevciK equation, Ip = (2.69 × 105) n3/2AD1/2 Cν1/2 [48], it was possible to estimate the electroactive surface area at 0.108 cm2, approximately 2-fold of that obtained for the bare SPE, 0.055 cm2.

The study of the influence of pH (3, 4, 5, 7, and 9) was performed by the technique of square wave voltammetry (SWV) on the oxidation process of CFX 25 μmol L−1, whose voltammograms are presented in Fig. 5 . The decrease in ionic hydrogen concentration in the BR buffer shifts the anodic peak potentials to lower potential values. The linear correlations between Ep and pH, whose straight lines for peaks I and II are expressed by, respectively, Ep = 0.061[pH] + 0.93, R2 = 0.97, and Ep = 0.059[pH] + 0.91, R2 = 0.97 and (Fig. 5(a)) showed slopes of 61 mV and 59 mV per pH unit. In the correlation plot between ip and pH (Fig. 5(b)), it is observed that in the pH range analyzed, the maximum value occurred at pH 5. This is the value assumed for the following analyses.

Using the Nernst equation, represented by Ep = - (0.0592 m/n) pH + b where the term 0.0592 m/n is equal to the angular coefficient of the line, m represents the number of protons and n the number of electrons [49,50], the ratio m/n is found. As such, it was concluded that for both peaks presented for the CFX oxidation process on the surface of the SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor, the ratio was 1:1. In this perspective, it was inferred that in peak I occurred, the transfer of one electron and one proton and that for peak II occurred the transfer of two electrons and two protons corroborating with the studies of Goyal et al. [51] and Shahrokhian et al. [19], respecting the electron/proton ratio calculated previously.

Therefore, it is assumed that the possible reaction scheme for the oxidation of CFX occurs at the aminothiazole substituent group, characteristic of the cephalosporins class, where the amino group of this substituent oxidizes with the loss of one hydrogen cation and one electron forming an imino radical (peak I) which subsequently includes dimer molecules. The second peak possibly refers to oxidation at the binding site where the dimmer molecules were formed, where the release of two electrons and two hydrogen cations occurs, enabling the formation of a double bond, as represented in Scheme 2 .

Next, the optimization process of the SWV technique parameters was performed in the presence of 50 μmol L−1 of CFX in 0.1 mol L−1 BR buffer (pH 5). The parameters studied were step potential, amplitude modulation, and frequency. Initially, analyses were performed varying the step potential at 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 mV. Among these, the best signal response obtained was at 4 mV. Next, the values 10, 20, 50, and 75 mV were studied for the modulation amplitude. Whose value that presented the highest Δip was 50 mV. Finally, analyses were performed at frequencies 1, 5, 10, and 25 Hz, with 25 Hz being the value that presented the highest signal response to CFX oxidation. Therefore, the optimized values were 4 mV for step potential, 50 mV for modulation amplitude, and 25 Hz for frequency (Fig. S5).

In addition, the influence of scan rates from 10 to 100 mVs−1 on the analytical response of the oxidation of 50 μmol L−1 of CFX was studied (Fig. S6). The process is characterized as irreversible by the positive variation of ip of CFX in BR buffer solution (pH 5). The correlation between ip furthermore the v1/2 was linear, indicating that the process is diffusion controlled [25,52] (Fig. S6(a)). In addition, the linear correlation between log ip and log v, whose slope value was 0.53, is presented, confirming that the oxidation of CFX on the surface of SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N is diffusion controlled [48] (Fig. S6(b)).

The electrochemical performance of the SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor in CFX detection was evaluated by the SWV technique at the previously optimized parameters. The obtained results are demonstrated in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6(a) shows the linear relationship between ip (μA) and [CFX] expressed by ip (μA) = 0.114[CFX] + 0.106 (R2 = 0.996) in the linear range from 0.0078 to 40 μmol L−1. The detection limit obtained was 0.2 nmol L−1, calculated by equation LOD = 3σ/b, where σ represents the average standard deviation of the blank (n = 10) and b the angular coefficient.

Table 1 compares the LOD of the sensor developed in this study and others already presented in the literature. According to the data presented in Table 1 and it is observed that the LOD obtained for the detection of CFX by the SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor was considered satisfactory since it showed higher sensitivity than those presented in the literature. It is noteworthy that the high sensitivity of the sensor is allied to the ease of production and disposability after analysis, avoiding poisoning of the electrode, as well as the compromising of sensitivity and reproducibility, which occurs in non-disposable electrodes such as those based on glassy carbon because they require constant cleaning. These are disadvantages presented by traditional electrodes [28,29]. In addition, the low-volume of 50 μL per analysis stands out, ensuring minimization of the sample volume required for the electrochemical detection of the antibiotic ceftriaxone.

The SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor stands out over hanging mercury drop electrode (HMDE) due to the high toxicity of mercury that makes it unviable due to the increasing need for environmental control and monitoring of these contaminants and bioaccumulative species. Moreover, among all the summarized sensors, the one proposed in this study presented lower oxidation potential, expanding its applicability when one highlights the lower energy expenditure and, consequently, minimization of interferences related to the potential range supported by the SPE for the oxidation process of CFX to occur.

In addition, repeatability was evaluated using five successive measurements with the same electrode. The relative standard deviation obtained was 5.54%. Finally, analyses were performed regarding reproducibility with eight different electrodes, and the RSD was 3.27% (Fig. S7). All these studies were performed with a CFX 25 μmol L−1 concentration in 0.1 mol L−1 BR buffer (pH 5).

The selectivity of SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor in the oxidation reaction of CFX antibiotic in different concentrations (5.0, 7.5, 10.0, and 15.0 μmol L−1) was determined in the presence of glucose (50.0 μmol L−1), uric acid (30.0 μmol L−1), ascorbic acid (30.0 μmol L−1) and caffeine (30.0 μmol L−1) considered as potentially interfering substances to this study. The results show that the variation in peak current (Δip) did not exceed 7.0% in the presence of the individual interferents for each antibiotic concentration analyzed. Thus, as observed in Fig. S8, the proposed sensor showed a good separation of the oxidation signal response compared to the ascorbic and uric acid responses. Furthermore, glucose and caffeine did not lead to interference, emphasizing that the signal referring to caffeine did not present itself in the potential range used for the proposed analyses. In addition, to investigate possible interferences cationic in the method, a standard solution of CFX antibiotic (10 μmol L−1 - reference) of was used and ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ Mn2+, Zn2+ Co2+ in concentrations 10-fold greater than the reference. The analyzes showed that these potential interferences do not significantly affect the selectivity of the sensor, with a variation of ±2.5%, as shown in Fig. S9. These analyzes were performed in triplicate.

Recovery analyses on real samples were performed on synthetic urine, urine, and tap water. The sample preparation is shown in the experimental topic. The synthetic urine and urine sample analyses showed recovery between 95%, 100% and 95%, 105%, respectively. In tap water, 98% and 105% were recovered. The data are shown in Table 2 and were obtained in triplicate. These results demonstrate that the SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor using SWV can be applied in the analysis of the antibiotic CFX since the results presented showed that the determination of the drug does not suffer a significant matrix effect.

The development of a new printed and disposable electrochemical platform for ceftriaxone antibiotic determination, named SPE/SiO2/ZrO2/Cdot-N sensor, was proposed in this study. The novel nanocomposite based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots, silicon oxide, and zirconium nanoparticles showed good adhesion to the SPE surface, synergistically contributing to the direct CFX oxidation reaction. The sensor showed high selectivity over a linear range from 0.0078 to 40.02 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 0.2 nmol L−1. The electrochemical method showed advantages in the direct determination of ceftriaxone antibiotic, which can be employed as a simple, low-cost, disposable, with low-volume sample used per analysis and, principally, a fast alternative for the CFX determination in real samples. In addition, we highlight the high selectivity of the developed method in the presence of possible interferences such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, uric acid, ascorbic acid, glucose, and caffeine since these substances showed no significant peak current variation.

Francisco Walison Lima Silva: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft preparation. Luccas L Name and Daniel Y Tiba: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Thiago C Canevari: Synthesis. Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Bernardo Ferreira Braz: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Ricardo Erthal Santelli: Writing – review & editing. Fernando Henrique Cincotto: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All the authors thank the Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior (CAPES), Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq) and Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado do Rio de Janeiro (FAPERJ) (Cincotto Proc. E-26/202.696/2019, Proc. E-26/010.002267/2019 and E-26/210.304/2022) for the financial support. Thiago Cruz Canevari thanks FINEP and Mackpesquisa for the financial support.

J. Electroanal. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Innovative strategy based on a novel carbon-black−β-cyclodextrin nanocomposite for the simultaneous determination of the anticancer drug flutamide and the environmental pollutant 4-nitrophenol
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kubendhiran, R. Sakthivel, S.-M. Chen, B. Mutharani, T.-W. Chen, Innovative Strategy Based on a Novel Carbon-Black−β-Cyclodextrin Nanocomposite for the Simultaneous Determination of the Anticancer Drug Flutamide and the Environmental Pollutant 4-Nitrophenol, Anal Chem. 90 (2018) 6283-6291. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.8b00989.

Sensor. Actuator. B Chem.

Ultrason. Sonochem.
